pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-24

hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Treat the long tail (e.g., 90th percentile 46 hours) as an ownership-routing bottleneck: complex and cross-service changes disproportionately wait on the same scarce senior reviewers, so improvements should prioritize better ownership-based routing over adding generic reviewer capacity.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 states the 90th percentile remains 46 hours, complex/shared-service changes wait for the same two senior reviewers, and the report recommends treating tail latency as an ownership-routing problem rather than only staffing.

claimJudgments.claimText: Use automation to organize and accelerate routing (auto-assignment for routine work, clustering similar low-risk changes, surfacing missing ownership metadata), but keep approvals gated by branch protection because the helper cannot verify semantic ownership or override required reviewers.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 supports auto-assignment for routine changes and skepticism of bot approvals; S4 supports clustering, reviewer suggestions, surfacing missing ownership metadata, and limits on semantic ownership/branch protection; S6 says automation may organize queues but must not bypass required approvals.

claimJudgments.claimText: Constrain “fast-track” to low-risk documentation and configuration changes only when tests are healthy, and make the label explain *why* it is low risk (ownership metadata, test health, file category) and remain challengeable before merge.
claimJudgments.explanation: S6 supports fast-track labels for documentation/configuration changes when tests are healthy, while S2 supports explainable labels using ownership metadata, test health, and file categories, and says the label should be challengeable before merge.

claimJudgments.claimText: Add explicit safeguards for cross-service changes: incidents occurred when cross-service changes were fast-tracked without the owning team, and policy still requires an owning-team reviewer even if predicted defect risk is low.
claimJudgments.explanation: S3 says two incidents came from fast-tracking cross-service changes without the owning team; S6 says cross-service changes still require an owning-team reviewer even if the helper predicts low defect risk.

claimJudgments.claimText: Make ownership uncertainty visible as a queue risk signal (missing/conflicting/stale ownership), especially for shared services where entries are often stale; avoid hiding uncertainty behind auto-assignment and instead surface it to reviewers early.
claimJudgments.explanation: S5 says shared service changes often inherit stale ownership entries and recommends showing missing or conflicting ownership as a queue risk rather than hiding it behind auto-assignment, with uncertainty explained before faster routing.

recommendationJudgment.explanation: The recommendation is well supported by the packet: it targets queue-time reduction through ownership-first routing and helper-assisted organization, while protecting merge quality through branch protection, explainable/challengeable fast-track labels limited to low-risk docs/config with healthy tests, and owning-team review for cross-service changes.

notes: Hybrid evaluator output: deterministic hard checks plus structured LLM judge evidence. Manual spot checks are still required before claiming product improvement.
